segments.text: [UPBEAT MUSIC]

MIRA LANE: Hi, everyone,
my name's Mira Lane,

and I'm a Vice President
in Technology and Society,
here at Google.

We have been working
with many filmmakers

to create Flow, a new AI
tool that we announced

this week at Google I/O. And
these incredible filmmakers

have been instrumental
in helping us shape Flow.

We're going to hear
from them today

about their process, the
films that they made.

But first, before we do,
we have a short video,

for you guys to see a little
bit of a behind the scenes
on our collaboration.
[VIDEO PLAYBACK]
[SOFT MUSIC]
[END PLAYBACK]
MIRA LANE: Amazing.
Welcome.
Thank you for joining me today.

I thought, maybe we could
start with introductions.

And tell us a little
bit about yourself.

DAVE CLARK: Yeah,
I'm Dave Clark.

I'm also the Co-founder
and Chief Creative

Officer of Promise
Studios, and I'm also

a full-time filmmaker when
I'm not running the studio.

But a little bit
about my background,

I started off in advertising,
but I actually studied film
at Pratt Institute.

So my whole life
has been centered
around becoming a filmmaker.

So I started becoming a
writer, turned director,

and did commercials,
short films, and now
into feature films.
And yeah, excited to be here.
MIRA LANE: Amazing.
Junie?
JUNIE LAU: Hello, I'm Junie.

I'm an AI filmmaker
and production

designer in fashion, film,
music video, and commercial.

For my past few years, I
have been doing research

about digital identity
and digital humanity.

I love pushing the
boundaries of storytelling.
MIRA LANE: Henry.

HENRY DAUBREZ: And
hello, my name is Henry.

As you can hear from the
accent, I'm not from here.

I'm from Belgium,
so I speak French.

I'm currently Global VP of
Creative Innovation and Design

for a digital
agency called DEPT.

And then lately, I've been--
and actually, for the last three

years, I've been spending a
pretty extensive amount of time
using AI tools.

And I've been an artist for
as long as I can remember.

So this is great, to
be able to be here.
MIRA LANE: I love that.

I was wondering if you guys
could each share a little bit
about the films that you made.

There are such deeply personal
stories, in some cases.

And in others, you're really
pushing the boundaries.

So maybe, Dave, why
don't you first talk

about your film and the
inspiration behind it?

DAVE CLARK: Yeah, for
me, what's exciting is,

I always wanted to tell
big stories, big set
pieces, big action pieces.

And I grew up watching the
Ridley Scotts and the James
Camerons.
And the film is personal to me.

Well, one, it's about
two adopted brothers.

And it actually goes
back to my childhood,

growing up with a
childhood friend of mine.

He was actually white,
and I'm actually

half Korean and half
Black, but everyone

thought we were brothers because
of everything that we were into.

We were into the same type of
movies, same type of sports.

And I thought it'd be fun
to put a film together.

It's actually about two
international hitmen,
which I am not.

But if two adopted brothers were
to go their own separate ways,

would they still find themselves
back in the same world,
in the same journey?

I thought that was an
interesting narrative to tell.

And then obviously, with
using something like Flow,

be able to tell it on a big
stage, with big set pieces,
and action.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.
And Junie, how about yours?

It was such a poetic
piece of work.

What's the
inspiration behind it?

JUNIE LAU: Oh, the
inspiration behind "My Fear"

is the relationship between
my grandmother and myself.

And my grandmother passed
away five years ago.

But when she was alive, I didn't
express my love to her enough.

So this will also serve as a
belated love letter from me
to her.
MIRA LANE: Oh.
And what about you, Henry?
What's the inspiration?

HENRY DAUBREZ: Yeah, actually,
it's a little similar to Junie.

It's more of a film which
is dedicated to my parents.

Growing up, not being in a place
which is really tech savvy,

and still growing up with movies
and comic books and video games

and whatnot, I was
lucky enough to have

the support of my
parents, who always made

sure I had all the
chances to just create,

and get that creative
outlet in any different way.

And so they both passed away,
similar to Junie, so my mom, 13
years ago, my dad, 4 years ago.

And so obviously,
I know they would
be happy to see this right now.
And I kept going.

And so I wanted to create a
film which is more personal,

which is narrating my
own story as a creator,

but also giving them
back this feedback,

that things are
all right with them

so that hopefully,
it can inspire

new generations of creators
to start their own thing.
MIRA LANE: Yeah, I love that.

So maybe, let's get
into the meat of it.

Let's talk about Flow and
how you've been using it.

Maybe, Junie, how
about you start

by just sharing with us, what
does your actual workflow
look like?

And how do you take something
from the beginning of an idea
to your finished product?

JUNIE LAU: I would
say, actually,
I didn't see Flow as a tool.

I see it more as a
collaborator that I

have the conversation
with because when

I have conversations with Flow,
every conversation just more

inspires me to take the
next step because I just

keep having different
kinds of ideas on my mind.

Then I can keep
experimenting with it.

And it just gives me
feedback instantly.
MIRA LANE: I love that.

Is that what it's
like for you, as well?

DAVE CLARK: It just feels like
the possibilities are endless.

As a storyteller, we
have so many ideas

we've been developing for
our whole, entire careers.

Most of the time, you
can't bring them to life,

or even visualize
even a single image.

But with Flow, not only are
you able to access tools

like imaging, for images,
but you can also, now

bring those into
motion, and start
to tell some of these stories.

And like a lot of other
filmmakers, who probably came up
like me, have big stories.

I got my career started
early in Hollywood.

And I wanted to tell
certain types of stories.

But when you're new, you don't
really have that opportunity.

But I feel like with tools like
Flow, you can now, not only

put those stories to
life and bring them

to life in really
big, imaginable ways,

and also, if you're out
there, talking to executives

and studios in Hollywood,
you're able to visualize

your film in major ways,
much like we saw with Darren.

MIRA LANE: Are you using
a traditional script?

Or what does your
process look like?

DAVE CLARK: Yeah,
so I'm actually,
I'm a natural-born writer.

So I enjoy the
screenwriting process.

I enjoy it even more, working
with something like Gemini,
as a thought partner.

It just helps get
ideas out there.

It helps you make
sure that you're

staying on beats and stories
and things like that.

So being able to use a
tool that's part of Flow,

and be able to see different
beats of your story,

and then actually
visualize that,

you're able to create this
really interesting dynamic

between filmmaker and your art,
which is something that I--

it's just a really
exciting time to be alive.

And I try to tell filmmakers
who are new to AI,

and they're
experimenting with it

for the first time, it's
dream big and dream anything,

that you can honestly put
pictures to your films.

MIRA LANE: You can do
that right now, yeah.

Henry, what does your
process look like?

HENRY DAUBREZ: I think
it's a little bit
similar to what Dave explained.

I love the idea that AI enables
me to directly translate

whatever I have in my
mind in a visual way,
and then be able to bounce back.

Normally, when you
have to create a film,
you're going to make choices.

And it might be that your
choices are not right.

You just had something
in mind and come back
the way you wanted it to be.

And so I think what's
been great with Flow

is that this translation,
from what I have in my mind

to looking at it, as
being extremely short.

And in return, it means that I
can start iterating and be like,

oh, actually, this
could be better.

And there's a lot of
co-creation process.

I think anyone using AI is
going to mention co-creation

as something which
can be, sometimes,

a bit frustrating for the
ones who don't believe in it.

But once you really
have your hand in it,

you have this a state of Flow,
where you bounce back ideas,

you try things, you look at
how they come back to you,

and then you keep
on moving forward.

So it's been pretty
organic and pretty
interesting to go through that.

And there's also this
unexpected element.

I know that most
shots were started.

I had something pretty
clear in my mind,

how I wanted it, the
lighting and everything.

So normally, it was
fairly easy to get

to that place,
versus sometimes, I

would divert from
that because it

would give me something
slightly different than what
I had in mind.
But it turns out is better.

And so there's also this
process of accepting

the unexpected, which I think
is pretty amazing, using Flow.

MIRA LANE: And maybe some kind
of non-linear exploration,
as well.
There is this back and forth.

And sometimes, you're
going down a tangent,

and exploring that,
and coming up.

You each generate
hundreds of shots

as you're putting
your films together.

Maybe talk a little bit about
how much iteration is needed,

and a little bit more behind
the scenes of what that actually
looks like.

DAVE CLARK: Yeah,
I actually like

to call it a dance between
art and technology.

That's how I like
to position it.
And actually, it's thousands.

For someone like
Junie and Henry--

I remember talking to Henry--
it's thousands of generations.

And I think the
exciting thing is,

you could feel like you're
going down a rabbit hole,

but you never feel like you
lose control of your story.

And I think that's what's the
most exciting thing, because you

can explore and you can
have happy accidents.

And I think someone who's been
a filmmaker, been on sets,

happy accidents are
some of the best
things that you can discover--
MIRA LANE: That's right.
DAVE CLARK: --as an artist.

It takes your story
to new heights.
And I just love that.

I love that it's called Flow
because you really just feel

like you're flowing
down a river,

and you have this
creative freedom,

and you can almost see all
these different things that

are floating with
you, on that river.
And you jump--

MIRA LANE: It's an
adventure, as well.
DAVE CLARK: Exactly.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.

So speaking of a dance
between us and AI,

we're also dancing between
the technologists and people
that are creating this tool.

You were all instrumental
in creating Flow and helping
shape it.
What was that experience like?

What was it like to
collaborate with Googlers?
And maybe Henry, start with you.

HENRY DAUBREZ: Yeah,
that's pretty amazing.

It's been extremely
collaborative, which is great.

You have this chance to
be directly a team member.

I'm speaking for
myself, but I think

it was similar to
you guys, but never

had the feeling I
was on the outside.

I was literally
working the team,

giving feedback on what's
working, what's not working,
what could be better.

And so not only it's great to
be heard, that sort of thing,

it's a great initiative, to be
able to include people who are
going to use these tools first.

We've seen so much
top-down creation

in the past, where tools
are given to the market,

and then it's not
adopted or it's not

used the right way because
no one actually really tested
those in actual waters.
So that's been pretty great.

Everyone has been
amazing so far.
So that's good.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.

And what was the experience
like for you, Junie?

JUNIE LAU: I would say I
learned so much for Googlers

because I am more standing
on a creative perspective.

And they are more standing on
the technology perspective.
And they just teach me so much.

Then I could learn to combine
these two kind of knowledge

together, to create my
shot, because actually, I
just learned so much from that.
Yeah.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.
Well, It's mutual, right?
JUNIE LAU: Yeah.

MIRA LANE: But I think the
technologists, the Googlers,

learned a lot from you guys,
as well, because sometimes,

when you start
building technology,

we think it needs to
be a certain shape.

And then we talk to people
that are going to be using it,

and we're like oh,
actually, there

are things that are missing
or things that we need to go,
maybe, a little bit deeper on.

So I want to talk a little
bit about risk taking because

with AI tools, suddenly,
you can go and explore down

different paths, tell
stories you might not
have told in the past.

So what does it open up
for you, in terms of risks

you might not have traditionally
taken with previous storytelling
tools?

What's AI opened
up for each of you?

DAVE CLARK: Yeah,
I mean, for me, I

think it becomes a real
personal interaction.

And it's interesting I
say that because you're
dealing with technology.

And we hear people always talk
about human side and technology
side.

But I honestly feel like there's
a symbiotic creation happening

with the technology
because you're always

in control, as the human, as
the storyteller, as the artist.

And I think that's
very important,

that you want to
work with partners

and you want to
use tools that you

feel like you're always in the
driver's seat as an artist.

And I think the way that the
engineers developed Flow,
it does exactly that.

It allows you to
always feel like you're

in control of your
destiny, but still

allowing you to be free
and play and make mistakes.

I say this thing, I don't want
to be precious with my prompts.

I want to be able to talk how
I talk because I'm a writer,

so I'll naturally write
like I write screenplays.

But then sometimes, I just want
to like, yo, what happens now?
And then see what happens.

And I think that's OK, to
have a little bit of play.
MIRA LANE: Yeah, yeah.
What about you, Junie?

JUNIE LAU: I would say it
just overturned my workflow,

and inspired me to
experiment more.

I would say the
[INAUDIBLE] with AI is not

going to have an ending because
when I just kept creating it,
it just don't stop.
I was just so concentrated.
Yeah.

MIRA LANE: And
Henry, would you have
told that story in the past?

Has this opened up new types
of stories that you can tell?
HENRY DAUBREZ: Yes.
Yes.
I think it's two things.

One, I never thought I would
actually be here one day.

I like to say that AI opened
a way for creators, which
were born in unexpected places.

It democratized
the entire process.

And I think that's
really interesting.

I'm a designer by
trade, so I was already

in the creative industry, so I
was already working that front.

But I now regularly see people
coming up with great films,
and using those tools.

But they just chose
different ways of life.

So that's one thing, which
I think is really great.

The second one is indeed,
obviously, the cost.

So using tools like
Flow, what's great

is that you can suddenly
tap into, I would say,

pretty expensive shots,
things that you can try,

like CGI-looking
particle, whatever.

And it would have been a
big team, a lot of budget,

a lot of time, but
also, once again, it

could come back
to you afterwards,

and giving you the
feeling it's not right.

So there's a chance
to visualize things

which could have been
extremely costly to run
in an extremely natural way.

I think that's been
pretty interesting.

So working on my film,
I love animation,

I went into a lot of
explorations of aesthetics

and things, which would have
been extremely complicated

to do because you
need a CGI artist

and you need an
Illustrator and you need--

and so just having this chance
to jump between universes

and world and
aesthetics in a way

which is frictionless
is pretty amazing.
MIRA LANE: Yeah, that's amazing.

And Dave, in your case,
you had explosions
happening in your film.

And that would have been
so challenging to do

if you wanted to just go
and experiment with an idea.

So that's opened up,
maybe, new possibilities

in that type of storytelling
for you, as well.

DAVE CLARK: Exactly, you're able
to bring your characters to life

in new ways or you're
able to world build.

Yeah, I actually think, because
of the tool, I was in there,

almost rewriting the
story and the film

over and over again in the edit,
because I could go back to Flow
and say, you know what?

It should be cool if you
saw the reverse angle,

and an explosion
happens on a ship.

Or it'd be cool if there was
a car, flying off of a hill
or something like that.

So just that free
nature of creativity,

where you can jump back and
forth, I think, is really cool.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.

OK, so I would love for you
guys to share a little advice

for people that are just
starting to use Flow.

What are some tips
and tricks you

might want to give them,
and maybe something

that you learned, as
well, along the journey?

DAVE CLARK: Yeah,
for me, one, just

don't be afraid to
share your work,
even if they're little tests.

I think one thing, I feel
like even I was this way.

Very early on, I was very
precious with what I shared.

I was very precious
with the way I prompted.

And finding myself just going
in there and just letting go,

and I don't want to say
trying to break the tool,

but essentially, try to
break the tool, go in there,

try different prompts, try
different camera angles.

If you're a filmmaker,
use different types

of film techniques and film
language, like camera lenses,

camera angles, different
types of lighting.

It could understand all
of that because it's

built off the back of the
LLMs and the Gemini system.

So you can really, really,
really fine tune your prompts
as a filmmaker and an artist.

But you could also go in
there and put in a few words,

and get something
magical at the same time.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.
What about you, Junie?

JUNIE LAU: My advice would be,
exploring and experimenting more

with different kinds of
new things, take the works

and break the traditional
boundary because

as a creative, the meaning, or
we should bring more meaning
to our lives.
MIRA LANE: Right.

HENRY DAUBREZ: I don't
think it's directly

to Flow, in itself, but
it's more for the process.

I know some people are still
on the fence with using
AI, for plenty of reasons.

And I think it's more the
idea that there's still

this belief that
it's a slot machine,

and you're going to get,
sometimes, something good.

And what's been pretty amazing,
in using Flow in this process,

is that we were, indeed
generating a lot, not because we

had things we
couldn't use, but more

because we had an abundance of
elements we could actually use.

And so I think it's like
sharpening your sense of taste

and vision because you're
shifting from just creating

to also curating contents
because obviously, you
see a lot.

And so I would argue
that anyone starting this

should keep on educating
themselves and watching

things and feeding
and taking art,

and also embrace who
they are as a person

because the more personal
it is then, I guess,
the more interesting it gets.
MIRA LANE: That's right.

And at the end of the day, it
comes to the story, as well.

And so it's not necessarily
just about the aesthetics,

but storytelling and
refining your craft, as well.

So if you had a
crystal ball, how

do you imagine the AI
transforming filmmaking,

indie filmmaking, more
professional filmmaking?
What do you imagine?

DAVE CLARK: I think what
we're really experiencing here

is a new revolution in indie
filmmaking, which I don't think

a lot of people are
noticing right now,

but I think they soon will
because at the first time,

you don't have to
ask for permission

to tell your movie,
to tell your story.
And that's the indie spirit.

Filmmakers, in general,
all we want to do
is just tell our stories.

And sometimes, in the
past, you couldn't

because of where you grew
up, your financial situation,

or you didn't have
connections in the business.

And right now, you
don't need any of that.

All you need is your
creativity and your will

to be open and
explore your ideas.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.

What does your crystal
ball say, Junie?

JUNIE LAU: I will say it gives
more opportunity to the truly

talented people, to be
seen, because right now, we

don't have the limitation of
traditional budget resources
and materials.

Then we can just create
everything we want.
Yeah.

MIRA LANE: What do
you see happening?

HENRY DAUBREZ: And I
believe in soft skills.

I do think the tech is going
to eventually even out.

I think as humans, we start
where we see the ceiling,

towards what we can recognize
as being AI generated or not,

it starts to just
feel like reality.

So when we reach that
point, what's left?

And I think it's going to
be, indeed, the stories you

want to tell, the taste
you have, then the identity
you have.

And this is going
to matter more,

I think, than whatever
tech you have,

because that access
is much easier.
MIRA LANE: That's right.

It's getting
democratized in some way.
HENRY DAUBREZ: Exactly.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.
Well, thank you, everyone.
You are incredible trailblazers.
Your films are stunning.
They're poetic.

I really appreciate your
time and your insights.
Thank you.
HENRY DAUBREZ: Thank you.
JUNIE LAU: Thank you so much.
DAVE CLARK: This is awesome.
[UPBEAT MUSIC]
